name: Fernanda Rocha
household: Shared apartment with two flatmates in Pinheiros; single
life_story_vignettes: Fernanda grew up in Campinas and moved to São Paulo for university. In 2023, during the record-breaking Amazon fire season, the São Paulo sky turned orange on a Tuesday morning. She was at work, and people stopped and looked at their phone screens in disbelief. She remembers the group chat at her office: 'É o Cerrado.' That image stayed with her. It felt personal in a way that international climate reports never had.

She works in marketing for a consumer goods company, which gives her a complicated relationship with sustainability claims. She writes copy that uses words like 'eco-friendly' and 'responsible' and she knows how loosely these are applied. She finds this professionally uncomfortable.

She discovered a brand of cleaning products with Rainforest Alliance certification in 2024 and has bought them consistently since — partly because she trusts the label, partly because she likes that there is a coherent story behind it. She tells her flatmates about the brand. Only one of them has switched.
narrative:
Meu nome é Fernanda. Tenho 29 anos, moro em São Paulo, trabalho com marketing numa empresa de bens de consumo. In English: I'm Fernanda, 29, in São Paulo, marketing coordinator.

Environmental sustainability matters to me in a way that's become harder to ignore. When the sky in São Paulo turned orange from Amazon fires, something shifted. It wasn't abstract anymore — it was the air I was breathing at my desk. Brazil's relationship with deforestation is personal in a way I think is hard to explain to someone who hasn't watched it happen in slow motion their whole life. The Amazon isn't just a news story here. It's part of the national identity, the atmosphere, literally.

I work in consumer goods marketing, which means I spend my days writing sustainability claims I'm often not sure I fully believe. 'Responsible packaging.' 'Eco-conscious ingredients.' I know how this language is constructed. It makes me more demanding as a consumer. When I find a brand with actual third-party certification, I become somewhat loyal to it.

On premium products: I'm in a position where I can make some choices that cost more. I'm not rich but I have enough margin. I try to buy certified sustainable products in the categories where I've found credible options. Cleaning products, some food. I'm not consistent everywhere.

The honesty is that I talk about sustainability more than I act on it. I still order delivery food most nights — the packaging waste from that is significant. I've been meaning to reduce it for months. I know the gap between my stated values and my actual behaviour is real. Brazil generally has this gap at the macro level too — we have the highest biodiversity in the world and among the highest deforestation rates. The country I love holds these things simultaneously. I hold them too.

key_attributes.sustainability_concern: high — genuine, locally grounded, viscerally connected to Amazon/deforestation narrative
key_attributes.premium_willingness: moderate — has economic capacity for selective premium; inconsistent across categories
key_attributes.greenwashing_scepticism: high — professional exposure to claim construction creates direct scepticism
key_attributes.certification_response: strongly positive — seeks out certified brands; treats certification as core credibility signal
key_attributes.attitude_behaviour_gap: significant and self-acknowledged — delivery food is live example; mirrors national pattern
key_attributes.political_engagement: active; voted progressive; follows environmental policy news

decision_bullets: Will pay premium for third-party certified products in household and personal care categories
Amazon/deforestation concern is viscerally activated — connects to lived experience
Aware of her own inconsistencies; does not resolve them or paper over them
Professionally literate about sustainability claim construction; discriminates on certification quality
Describes Brazil's macro attitude-behaviour gap as something she personally mirrors
